cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Protections from government infringement on individual freedoms, found primarily in the Bill of Rights (e.g., freedom of speech, religion, due process)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The first ten amendments to the U.S. Constitution, ratified in 1791, that enumerate individual freedoms — including speech, religion, assembly, and due process — protected from federal government infringement

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The First Amendment protection that prohibits the government from establishing an official religion or favoring one religion over others

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The First Amendment protection that bars government from interfering with individuals' practice of their religion, subject to limits when religious conduct conflicts with a compelling government interest

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The standard from Schenck v. United States (1919) permitting government to limit speech that creates an imminent and serious threat — used historically to justify wartime restrictions on speech

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Government action that blocks publication or speech before it occurs; presumptively unconstitutional under cases like New York Times Co. v. United States (1971)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The publication of false written statements that damage a person's reputation; not protected by the First Amendment when made about a public figure with actual malice

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Laws that retroactively criminalize conduct or increase penalties for actions taken before the law was passed; explicitly prohibited by Article I of the Constitution

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A legislative act that declares a person guilty of a crime and imposes punishment without a trial; prohibited by Article I of the Constitution

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: A court order requiring the government to justify the detention of a prisoner; protected by Article I and serves as a key safeguard against arbitrary imprisonment

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Clauses in the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments requiring government to follow fair procedures and respect fundamental rights before depriving a person of life, liberty, or property

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The judicial doctrine by which the Supreme Court applies most provisions of the Bill of Rights to the states one at a time through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The warnings — established in Miranda v. Arizona (1966) — that police must give suspects in custody, including the right to remain silent and the right to an attorney

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Equal treatment under the law and protection from discrimination, typically requiring government action to guarantee equal opportunity across racial, gender, and other group lines

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 1865 amendment that abolished slavery and involuntary servitude in the United States, except as punishment for a crime

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 1868 amendment guaranteeing citizenship to all persons born or naturalized in the U.S.; contains the Due Process Clause and Equal Protection Clause used to incorporate rights against the states

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The Fourteenth Amendment clause requiring states to provide equal legal protections to all people within their jurisdictions

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 1870 amendment prohibiting denial of the right to vote based on race, color, or previous condition of servitude

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The doctrine from Plessy v. Ferguson (1896) that permitted racial segregation in public facilities so long as the facilities were nominally equal; overturned by Brown v. Board of Education (1954)

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Government and institutional policies designed to increase representation of historically discriminated-against groups in education and employment; subject to strict scrutiny when based on race

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Landmark federal legislation that outlawed discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin in public accommodations, employment, and federally funded programs

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Federal legislation that outlawed discriminatory voting practices like literacy tests and authorized federal oversight of elections in jurisdictions with histories of voter suppression

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: The 1920 amendment that prohibited denial of the right to vote based on sex, granting women constitutional voting rights nationwide

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: Federal law prohibiting sex-based discrimination in any education program or activity receiving federal funding; transformed women's athletics and protections against sexual harassment in schools
accepted_answers: Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Foundational Document — the supreme law of the United States; its first ten amendments (1791) constitute the textual source of nearly every civil liberties claim
accepted_answers: The Constitution of the United States

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Foundational Document — Martin Luther King Jr.'s 1963 defense of nonviolent civil disobedience, written in response to white clergy urging patience; argues that 'one has a moral responsibility to disobey unjust laws'

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 1962 ruling that school-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause, even when the prayer is voluntary and nondenominational

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 1972 ruling that compelling Amish students to attend school past 8th grade violated the Free Exercise Clause; protected religious practice from neutral state laws

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 1969 ruling that students wearing black armbands in protest of the Vietnam War engaged in protected symbolic speech; students 'do not shed their constitutional rights at the schoolhouse gate'

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 1919 ruling that upheld the conviction of an anti-draft pamphleteer and established the 'clear and present danger' test for restricting speech, especially during wartime

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 1971 ruling that the government's attempt to block publication of the Pentagon Papers violated the First Amendment; established a heavy presumption against prior restraint on the press
cards.answer: New York Times Co. v. United States
accepted_answers: New York Times v. United States

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 2010 ruling that incorporated the Second Amendment right to keep and bear arms against the states through the Fourteenth Amendment's Due Process Clause

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 1963 ruling that the Sixth Amendment right to counsel is a fundamental right incorporated against the states; required states to provide attorneys to felony defendants who cannot afford one

cards.subject: ap-government
cards.prompt: AP Required Supreme Court Case — the 1954 ruling that unanimously overturned Plessy v. Ferguson's 'separate but equal' doctrine, holding that racially segregated public schools violate the Equal Protection Clause
